content.text: Data Residency Audit Evidence and Attestation

Demonstrating data residency compliance is not the same as implementing it. Regulators, enterprise customers exercising audit rights, ISO 27001 auditors, and internal CISO review boards all require written, verifiable evidence that personal data has stayed within the contracted geographic boundary. For Gen AI workloads, that evidence must cover every hop: the model endpoint, the vector store, the embedding pipeline, and the audit log itself.

This topic explains what evidence to collect, how to structure it into an audit package, how to attest that the package is complete and untampered, and how to maintain the programme across the annual regulatory cycle.

attrs.alt: Residency audit evidence collection and attestation workflow

content.text: What Residency Audit Evidence Must Cover
A regulator issuing a formal inquiry under Art. 58 GDPR or an enterprise customer invoking contractual audit rights will expect six categories of evidence:
Provider residency commitment letter or ISO 27001 SoA scoped to the specific region
Immutable NDJSON log with endpoint URL, region code, and data classification label for every GenAI call
SOC 2 Type II (availability + confidentiality TSCs) and ISO 27001 certificate scoped to the data-centre region
Signed attestation that the pen-test covered the residency boundary network controls
Signed DPO letter confirming Article 30 records reflect actual processing locations
Every incident where data left the contracted region, with root cause, remediation, and impact assessment
Audit Log Schema for GenAI Requests

The most operationally-specific evidence is the per-request routing log. Every inference call must write a record that, together, demonstrate 100% routing compliance across the audit period. The record must be immutable — written once, never updated, append-only — so that the log cannot be retroactively altered to hide a residency violation.

Manually assembling audit evidence from multiple cloud portals is error-prone and slow. A programmatic evidence collector reduces preparation time from weeks to hours and eliminates the risk of missing an artifact at audit time.

Third-party audit reports are the most credible artifacts in a residency package. To be accepted as residency evidence they must be scoped correctly — a SOC 2 Type II report that covers a provider's global infrastructure does not prove your specific tenant data stayed in the EU. You must extract or obtain the region-specific annex.
Bridge letter + availability TSC section scoped to EU region
Data-centre name or Azure region code must appear in scope statement
Certificate with annex listing specific cloud regions in scope
Certificate scope description must include the region; SoA excerpt showing A.11.1 (physical access)
ATO letter for GovCloud US or equivalent
System boundary must include the LLM endpoint region
BAA with covered services enumerated; SAR if available
Services list must include the GenAI model endpoint and vector store
AOC or ROC Section 2 (scope) + Section 12.8 (third-party management)
Cardholder data environment must not overlap GenAI inference region unless scoped in

Evidence of residency compliance requires more than showing the logs exist — it requires demonstrating that the logs cover the entire audit period with no gaps, and that the compliance rate (requests correctly routed) meets the contractual threshold, which is typically 100% for regulated data and 99.9% for internal data under an SLA grace model.

Any routing violation — a request for EU data subjects that reached a US endpoint, a vector-store write that bypassed the regional selector — must be documented as an exception even if the root cause was a transient infrastructure fault and the data was not compromised. Regulators treat undocumented exceptions more harshly than documented ones, because an undocumented exception suggests systemic control failure.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class Severity(str, Enum):
    P1 = "P1"   # Regulated data crossed border: notify DPA within 72 h (GDPR Art. 33)
    P2 = "P2"   # Confidential data crossed border: internal escalation
    P3 = "P3"   # Internal data: log + remediate within SLA
    P4 = "P4"   # Public data: log only

class ExceptionStatus(str, Enum):
    OPEN        = "open"
    REMEDIATED  = "remediated"
    CLOSED      = "closed"
    DPA_NOTIFIED = "dpa_notified"

@dataclass
class ResidencyException:
    exception_id:       str
    detected_utc:       datetime
    severity:           Severity
    affected_region:    str             # region where data should have stayed
    actual_region:      str             # region where data actually went
    request_ids:        list[str]       # routing log IDs for cross-reference
    root_cause:         str
    data_categories:    list[str]       # e.g. ["PII", "PHI"]
    estimated_records:  int             # approximate number of data subject records
    status:             ExceptionStatus = ExceptionStatus.OPEN
    remediation_steps:  list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    closed_utc:         Optional[datetime] = None
    dpa_notification_ref: Optional[str] = None

    def dpa_notification_required(self) -> bool:
        return self.severity == Severity.P1 and "PII" in self.data_categories

    def notification_deadline_utc(self) -> Optional[datetime]:
        """72-hour GDPR Art. 33 deadline from detection time."""
        if self.dpa_notification_required():
            from datetime import timedelta
            return self.detected_utc + timedelta(hours=72)
        return None

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        deadline = self.notification_deadline_utc()
        if deadline is None:
            return False
        return datetime.utcnow() > deadline and self.status != ExceptionStatus.DPA_NOTIFIED
REGULATED or CONFIDENTIAL data left contracted region
DPA within 72 h (Art. 33 GDPR); customer within 48 h (DPA clause)
Immediate isolation; root-cause report within 5 days
Root cause within 48 h; control fix within 14 days
Root cause within 5 days; control fix within 30 days
PUBLIC data routed to wrong region (no residency obligation)
Informational log; trend review at next quarterly review

The most human element of the audit package is the Data Protection Officer's attestation letter. Unlike automated evidence, this letter vouches for completeness of the Article 30 Records of Processing Activities (ROPA) and confirms that the processing locations documented in the ROPA match what the technical controls enforce. It is typically one to two pages and covers:
Confirmation that the Gen AI processing activities are recorded in the ROPA with correct data-centre regions
Statement that the DPO has reviewed the routing-log coverage report and found no unexplained residency gaps in the audit period
Acknowledgement of any P1 or P2 exceptions documented in the exception register, with confirmation that DPA notifications were made within the required window
Reference to the cloud provider residency commitments (sub-processor agreements listing the specific data-centre locations)
DPO name, contact, and signature date (and, where required, registration number with the national supervisory authority)

Practitioner note: The DPO letter should be drafted by legal or compliance, not by engineering. The technical team's role is to produce the coverage report and exception register so the DPO has verifiable numbers to sign off on, not to generate the attestation text itself.

The following pattern assembles and delivers the audit package. In practice, the scratchpad builder runs in a CI job triggered on a schedule (quarterly or on-demand), generates the manifest and coverage report, and attaches them to the encrypted ZIP before uploading to the secure document-sharing channel.

from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date
from evidence_registry import EvidenceRegistry, EvidenceArtifact  # from earlier

def assemble_annual_audit_package(
    evidence_dir: Path,
    output_dir: Path,
    period_start: date,
    period_end: date,
) -> Path:
    registry = EvidenceRegistry()

    # Each file in evidence_dir follows naming convention: <category>__<region>__<date>.ext
    for artifact_path in sorted(evidence_dir.iterdir()):
        if not artifact_path.is_file() or artifact_path.suffix == ".sha256":
            continue
        parts = artifact_path.stem.split("__")
        if len(parts) < 3:
            raise ValueError(f"Artifact filename does not follow convention: {artifact_path.name}")
        category, region, collected_date_str = parts[0], parts[1], parts[2]
        artifact = EvidenceArtifact(
            artifact_id    = artifact_path.stem,
            category       = category,
            filename       = artifact_path.name,
            description    = f"{category} for region {region}",
            region         = region,
            collected_date = date.fromisoformat(collected_date_str),
            path           = artifact_path,
        )
        registry.register(artifact)

    coverage = registry.coverage_report()
    if not coverage["is_audit_ready"]:
        missing = coverage["categories_missing"]
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Audit package incomplete. Missing categories: {missing}. "
            "Collect these artifacts before delivering the package."
        )

    output_zip = output_dir / f"residency_audit_{period_start}_{period_end}.zip"
    registry.build_audit_package(output_zip, period_start, period_end)
    return output_zip

An annual audit package answers a point-in-time question: 'Was data residency maintained across the last 12 months?' A continuous attestation programme answers a real-time question: 'Is data residency currently enforced?' Enterprise customers increasingly prefer continuous attestation — a live dashboard or API endpoint that returns current compliance status — over the traditional annual PDF.
Encrypted ZIP with manifest and DPO letter
Low — batch job once per year
Automated coverage report PDF + routing log sample
Live API endpoint returning compliance metrics and SLA status
Rare — accepted by some DPAs for consent-based queries
Signed JSON Web Token per request proving routing decision
Very high — PKI per region, token storage

Most enterprise programmes operate at the quarterly rolling cadence: automated coverage reports are generated monthly, reviewed by the DPO quarterly, and packaged annually for formal delivery. This gives early warning of coverage gaps before they accumulate into an audit-period failure.
Routing log chain integrity passes verify_chain()
Coverage report shows 0 violations or all violations documented
New cloud-provider residency attestation PDFs collected
SOC 2 Type II period report received from provider
Exception register reviewed and P1/P2 notifications confirmed
DPO attestation letter signed for current audit period
Audit package ZIP generated and hash published to customers
ROPA entries reconciled against actual routing configuration

Data residency audit evidence is the documentary proof that backs every technical control described in the residency controls programme. It spans cloud provider attestations, immutable per-request routing logs with SHA-256 hash chaining, SOC 2 and ISO 27001 reports scoped to the exact data-centre region, DPO attestation letters, and a documented exception register for every routing violation. The annual audit package bundles these into a manifest-indexed, checksum-verified ZIP. Automation makes the programme sustainable: daily chain verification, weekly coverage reports, and scripted package assembly reduce the manual burden so that the DPO's sign-off remains the only non-automated gate.
